---
output: github_document
---

<!-- README.md is generated from README.Rmd. Please edit that file -->

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.path = "man/figures/README-",
  fig.width = 8,
  fig.height = 5,
  dpi = 300
)
devtools::load_all()
```

# fixes <a><img src="man/figures/fixes_logo_blue.png" align="right" height="138" width="120" /></a>

<!-- badges: start -->
[![CRAN status](https://www.r-pkg.org/badges/version/fixes)](https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=fixes)
[![R-CMD-check](https://github.com/yo5uke/fixes/actions/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/yo5uke/fixes/actions/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml)
<!-- badges: end -->

## Overview

`fixes` is an R package for **event study analysis** in panel data — the workhorse tool for visualizing parallel trends and dynamic treatment effects in difference-in-differences research.

Version 0.10.0 supports six estimators for panel and repeated cross-section data,
all accessible through the same `run_es()` interface.

**Key functions:**

| Function | What it does |
|---|---|
| `run_es()` | Estimate an event study (6 estimators available) |
| `plot_es()` | Static ggplot2 event study plot |
| `plot_att_gt()` | Visualize the full ATT(g,t) matrix (CS estimator) |
| `plot_es_interactive()` | Interactive plotly plot with hover tooltips |

**Estimators** (selected via the `estimator` argument in `run_es()`):

| `estimator` | Reference | Data structure | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| `"twfe"` | Classic TWFE | Panel | Universal treatment timing |
| `"cs"` | Callaway & Sant'Anna (2021) | Panel | Staggered adoption |
| `"sa"` | Sun & Abraham (2021) | Panel | Staggered adoption |
| `"bjs"` | Borusyak, Jaravel & Spiess (2024) | Panel | Staggered adoption |
| `"twm"` | Wooldridge (2025) | Panel | Staggered adoption; optional cohort-specific trends (`trends = TRUE`) |
| `"flex"` | Deb, Norton, Wooldridge & Zabel (2024) | Repeated cross-section | RCS data where the same unit is not tracked over time |

## Installation

```r
# From CRAN
install.packages("fixes")

# Development version
pak::pak("yo5uke/fixes")
```

## Quick start

```{r load-library, eval=FALSE}
library(fixes)
```

All six estimators share the same interface:

```{r all-estimators, eval=FALSE}
# Classic TWFE (single treatment date)
res_twfe <- run_es(
  data = df,
  outcome = y,
  treatment = treat,
  time = period,
  timing = 5,
  fe = ~ id + period
)

# Sun & Abraham (2021) — staggered adoption
res_sa <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  fe = ~ id + year,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "sa"
)

# Callaway & Sant'Anna (2021) — staggered adoption
res_cs <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "cs",
  control_group = "nevertreated"
)

# Borusyak, Jaravel & Spiess (2024) — staggered adoption
res_bjs <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "bjs"
)

# Wooldridge (2025) Two-Way Mundlak — staggered adoption
res_twm <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  fe = ~ id + year,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "twm"
)

# Deb et al. (2024) FLEX — repeated cross-section data
res_flex <- run_es(
  data = df_rcs,          # data with different individuals each period
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,        # first treatment period; NA = never treated
  group = group_id,       # treatment group identifier (R_{ig} in Deb et al.)
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "flex"
)
```

---

## Classic event study (single treatment date)

Use `run_es()` with a fixed event date. Here we use `fixest::base_did`, a
simulated balanced panel where all units are treated at period 5.

```{r classic-run}
df <- fixest::base_did

es <- run_es(
  data = df,
  outcome = y, # outcome variable (unquoted)
  treatment = treat, # 0/1 treatment indicator
  time = period, # time variable
  timing = 5, # treatment occurs at period 5
  fe = ~ id + period,
  cluster = ~id,
  baseline = -1 # period -1 is the reference (default)
)

print(es)
```

```{r classic-plot}
plot_es(es)
```

---

## Staggered adoption

When units adopt treatment at different times, the classic TWFE estimator can
be biased. `fixes` provides three modern alternatives.

**Setup:** create a shared dataset from `fixest::base_stagg`.
Never-treated units use `NA` for their timing column — this is the convention
for all three staggered estimators.

```{r stagg-setup}
df_stagg <- fixest::base_stagg
df_stagg$timing <- df_stagg$year_treated
df_stagg$timing[df_stagg$year_treated == 10000] <- NA # mark never-treated
```

### Callaway & Sant'Anna (2021) — `estimator = "cs"`

Estimates a separate ATT(g,t) for every combination of cohort g and calendar
time t, then aggregates to the event-study curve. Comparison group can be
never-treated units (default) or not-yet-treated units.

```{r cs-run}
cs <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing, # first treatment period; NA = never treated
  unit = id,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "cs",
  control_group = "nevertreated" # or "notyettreated"
)

print(cs)
```

```{r cs-plot}
plot_es(cs)
```

#### Visualise the full ATT(g,t) matrix

`plot_att_gt()` shows every cohort × calendar-time cell, making it easy to
spot anticipation effects or heterogeneous dynamics.

```{r cs-heatmap}
plot_att_gt(cs, type = "heatmap")
```

```{r cs-facet}
plot_att_gt(cs, type = "facet")
```

---

### Sun & Abraham (2021) — `estimator = "sa"`

Builds cohort × horizon interaction terms, then aggregates with cohort-share
weights. Gives the same result as `fixest::sunab()` but through the unified
`run_es()` interface.

```{r sa-run}
sa <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  treatment = treated,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  fe = ~ id + year,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "sa",
  cluster = ~id
)

print(sa)
```

```{r sa-plot}
plot_es(sa)
```

---

### Borusyak, Jaravel & Spiess (2024) — `estimator = "bjs"`

A three-step imputation approach:

1. Fit a TWFE model on **untreated** observations only (never-treated + not-yet-treated).
2. Impute each treated unit's counterfactual outcome.
3. Average the imputed treatment effects by event-study horizon.

```{r bjs-run}
bjs <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "bjs"
)

print(bjs)
```

```{r bjs-plot}
plot_es(bjs)
```

---

### Wooldridge (2025) Two-Way Mundlak — `estimator = "twm"`

The Two-Way Mundlak (TWM) estimator (Wooldridge 2025, Procedure 5.1) is
algebraically equivalent to the Sun-Abraham estimator in the no-covariate base
case. Its main extensions are:

- **`trends = TRUE`**: adds cohort-specific linear trend regressors (`d_g × t`)
  to absorb differential pre-trends (Wooldridge 2025, Section 8). Uses
  post-treatment cells only; pre-treatment periods identify the trend. Output
  shows `relative_time ≥ 0` only.
- **`covariates = ~ x1 + x2`**: full Procedure 5.1 covariate interactions with
  cohort-demeaned treatment cells (`ẋ_ig = x_i − x̄_g`) and `i(time, x_j)`
  conditional parallel-trends controls.

```{r twm-run}
twm <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  fe = ~ id + year,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "twm"
)

print(twm)
```

```{r twm-plot}
plot_es(twm)
```

---

### Deb, Norton, Wooldridge & Zabel (2024) FLEX — `estimator = "flex"`

The FLEX estimator is designed for **repeated cross-section (RCS)** data where
different individuals are observed in each period (no unit tracking). Unlike
panel estimators, FLEX uses group fixed effects (`R_{ig}`) instead of unit
fixed effects.

Key requirements:

- Each observation belongs to a treatment **group** (`group` argument): all
  individuals with the same `group` value share a treatment timing.
- `NA` timing marks never-treated groups.
- Covariates (`covariates = ~ x1`) use group × time cell-level centering
  (Deb et al. 2024, Eq. 2.11).

```{r flex-run, eval=FALSE}
# df_rcs: one row per individual-period, different individuals each period.
# group_id identifies the treatment group each individual belongs to.
flex <- run_es(
  data = df_rcs,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,   # cohort value for the group; NA = never treated
  group = group_id,  # treatment group identifier (required for FLEX)
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "flex"
)

plot_es(flex)
```

---

## Bootstrap simultaneous confidence bands

Pointwise confidence intervals control error rates **one period at a time**.
When you display 15 pre- and post-treatment estimates on a single plot, the
chance that at least one interval incorrectly excludes zero can be much higher
than the nominal 5%.

**Simultaneous** bands (Callaway & Sant'Anna 2021, Corollary 1) provide joint
coverage: with probability ≥ 1 − α, the entire event-study curve is contained
within the band.

Use `bootstrap = TRUE` in `run_es()` and `show_simultaneous = TRUE` in
`plot_es()`:

```{r boot-run, eval=FALSE}
cs_boot <- run_es(
  data = df_stagg,
  outcome = y,
  time = year,
  timing = timing,
  unit = id,
  staggered = TRUE,
  estimator = "cs",
  control_group = "nevertreated",
  bootstrap = TRUE, # run multiplier bootstrap (Algorithm 1, CS 2021)
  B = 999, # number of bootstrap draws; 999 recommended in practice
  boot_seed = 42 # for reproducibility
)
```

```{r boot-plot, eval=FALSE}
# Lighter outer band = simultaneous CI; darker inner band = pointwise CI
plot_es(cs_boot, show_simultaneous = TRUE)
```

The simultaneous band is always at least as wide as the pointwise band. The
interactive plot also gains a second ribbon trace:

```{r boot-interactive, eval=FALSE}
plot_es_interactive(cs_boot, show_simultaneous = TRUE)
```

---

## Plotting options

`plot_es()` works with results from any estimator.

```{r plot-options}
# Error bars instead of ribbon
plot_es(es, type = "errorbar")
```

```{r plot-ci}
# Multiple CI levels at once
es_multi <- run_es(
  data = df,
  outcome = y,
  treatment = treat,
  time = period,
  timing = 5,
  fe = ~ id + period,
  cluster = ~id,
  conf.level = c(0.90, 0.95, 0.99)
)
plot_es(es_multi, ci_level = 0.90, theme_style = "minimal")
```

### Interactive plots

`plot_es_interactive()` produces a plotly chart with hover tooltips (requires
the `plotly` package).

```{r plot-interactive, eval=FALSE}
plot_es_interactive(es)
```

---

## Key `run_es()` arguments

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `data` | — | Data frame (panel or RCS) |
| `outcome` | — | Outcome variable (unquoted) |
| `treatment` | `NULL` | 0/1 treatment dummy (`"twfe"` only) |
| `time` | — | Time variable (numeric) |
| `timing` | — | Treatment date (scalar for `"twfe"`, column for others; `NA` = never treated) |
| `unit` | `NULL` | Unit ID column (required for `"cs"`, `"sa"`, `"bjs"`, `"twm"`) |
| `fe` | `NULL` | Fixed effects formula, e.g. `~ id + year` |
| `estimator` | `"twfe"` | `"twfe"`, `"cs"`, `"sa"`, `"bjs"`, `"twm"`, or `"flex"` |
| `staggered` | `FALSE` | Set `TRUE` for unit-varying treatment timing |
| `group` | `NULL` | **FLEX only**: unquoted group identifier (`R_{ig}`, required for `"flex"`) |
| `trends` | `FALSE` | **TWM only**: add cohort-specific linear trends (Wooldridge 2025, Section 8) |
| `covariates` | `NULL` | Covariate formula, e.g. `~ x1 + x2` (supported for `"twm"` and `"flex"`) |
| `control_group` | `"nevertreated"` | CS only: `"nevertreated"` or `"notyettreated"` |
| `cluster` | `NULL` | Clustering formula, e.g. `~ id` |
| `baseline` | `-1` | Reference period (0 = treatment date) |
| `lead_range` | auto | Pre-treatment periods to show |
| `lag_range` | auto | Post-treatment periods to show |
| `conf.level` | `0.95` | CI level(s); vector allowed, e.g. `c(0.90, 0.95)` |
| `vcov` | `"HC1"` | VCOV type (any `fixest::vcov()` type) |
| `bootstrap` | `FALSE` | CS only: run multiplier bootstrap for simultaneous CIs |
| `B` | `999` | CS bootstrap: number of draws |
| `boot_seed` | `NULL` | CS bootstrap: RNG seed for reproducibility |

---

## Contributing

Found a bug or have a feature request? Open an issue on
[GitHub](https://github.com/yo5uke/fixes/issues).
